name: 馬格努斯·博可

work_experience.position: Various roles including CFO, COO, President of OMX Technology division

person_relationships.description: Preceded Hsieh Fu Hua as CEO of SGX in 2009
Succeeded Bocker as CEO of SGX in 2015

major_achievements.achievement: Became CEO of OMX and built it into the world's largest provider of technology solutions for exchanges and clearing organizations
Orchestrated the merger of OMX with Nasdaq, creating Nasdaq OMX Group (now Nasdaq Inc.), one of the world's largest exchange operators
Appointed President of Nasdaq following the OMX-Nasdaq merger
Led a US$8.3 billion bid to acquire the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX), which was blocked by the Australian government in April 2011 on national interest grounds
Served as Chairman of the WFE Governance and Nomination Committee from 2012 to 2015
Became Honorary Chairman of SIAS, the shareholder advocacy group, after leaving SGX
Inducted into the FIA Futures Hall of Fame posthumously in 2019

biography_summary: Magnus Bocker (31 August 1961 - 26 July 2017) was a Swedish businessman and exchange industry leader. Born in Skollersta, Sweden, he graduated from Stockholm University with a degree in business in 1986 and joined OMX the same year. Over 17 years at OMX, he held various roles including CFO, COO, and President of the OMX Technology division before becoming CEO in 2003. Under his leadership, OMX became the world's largest provider of exchange technology solutions. He orchestrated the landmark merger of OMX with Nasdaq in 2008 and served as President of Nasdaq before being recruited as CEO of the Singapore Exchange (SGX) in December 2009. At SGX, he led an ambitious but ultimately unsuccessful US$8.3 billion bid for the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) in 2010-2011. After leaving SGX in 2015, he founded Blibros Capital Partners and co-founded Tryb, both focused on fintech investments. He also served as Honorary Chairman of SIAS. He died of cancer on 26 July 2017 in St. Louis, Missouri, at the age of 55.

collection_meta.notes: Enriched with detailed career timeline from Wikipedia, FIA Hall of Fame, Business Times, and Reuters. Added ASX bid details, board memberships, and LinkedIn. No family information found in public sources. Date of death and birth place corrected from Wikipedia (born Skollersta, died St. Louis).
